title: Why your heart races at rest when your nervous system is dysregulated
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The ghost in the machine 

 You're lying in bed. It's 11 PM. The room is dark, quiet, safe. Nothing is chasing you. No one is yelling. There's no deadline...
htmlContent:
The ghost in the machine

You're lying in bed. It's 11 PM. The room is dark, quiet, safe. Nothing is chasing you. No one is yelling. There's no deadline, no threat, no emergency. And yet your heart is pounding like you just ran from a bear.

I know this experience. I've lived it more nights than I can count. That feeling of your chest thumping against the mattress, your pulse visible in your throat, your body screaming RUN while your mind insists there's nothing wrong. It's confusing. It's frightening. And it's one of the most common signs that your nervous system is dysregulated.

Here's the thing. Your heart doesn't race because something is wrong right now. It races because something was wrong back then. Or because your nervous system learned, somewhere along the way, that safety isn't real. That rest is dangerous. That letting your guard down means getting hurt.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you this is your autonomic nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. Your body is scanning for safety and danger at all times, below the level of conscious thought. It's called neuroception. And when your neuroception gets stuck in a threat-detection loop, your heart keeps revving even when the coast is clear.

Think about it. Your heart rate at rest should be around 60 to 100 beats per minute. But when your nervous system is dysregulated, that baseline creeps up. You might sit still for an hour and still feel your pulse in your temples. You might wake up with your heart already pounding before your brain even registers being awake. That's not anxiety in the psychological sense. That's your vagus nerve sending distress signals from your gut to your heart to your brain, creating a feedback loop that won't shut off.

I remember lying on my couch one Sunday afternoon, completely still, and feeling my heart thud against my ribs like a trapped bird. I checked my phone. No bad news. I checked my thoughts. Nothing scary. I checked my body and found only tension, bracing, waiting. My system was preparing for a threat that didn't exist. And that's when I realized my body had its own timeline, its own memory, its own logic that didn't care about my rational mind.

Why rest feels like a setup

Here's what I've learned from watching my own nervous system and from reading people like Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score." When you've experienced trauma, especially repeated or developmental trauma, your nervous system rewires itself for survival. It learns that the world is dangerous, that people are unpredictable, that rest is when the bad things happen.

Think about it. If you grew up in a house where you had to stay hypervigilant to survive, where relaxation meant getting caught off guard by a parent's rage or a sudden crisis, then your body learned a crucial lesson. Rest is a trap. Safety is an illusion. The only way to stay alive is to stay alert.

So years later, in a completely safe environment, your body still follows those old rules. Your heart races at rest because rest itself feels threatening. Your nervous system doesn't know the trauma is over. It only knows that the last time you let your guard down, something bad happened. And it's not going to make that mistake again.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing. Levine's work shows how trauma gets trapped in the body, in the nervous system, in the way your heart beats and your muscles hold and your breath shortens. His approach isn't about talking through your memories. It's about letting your body complete the survival responses that got interrupted, so your system can finally rest.

I've spent years trying to understand why my heart races when I'm doing nothing. And I've come to see it as a kind of loyalty. My body is loyal to the past. It's trying to protect me from dangers that no longer exist. It's doing its job. But its job description is outdated.

The vagus nerve and the brake system

Your heart rate is controlled by two branches of your autonomic nervous system. The sympathetic branch is the gas pedal. It speeds your heart up when you need to fight or flee. The parasympathetic branch, specifically the vagus nerve, is the brake. It slows your heart down when you're safe.

When your nervous system is dysregulated, the brake stops working properly. Your vagus nerve loses tone, like a muscle that's been underused or overstressed. And without a functional brake, your heart just keeps revving. Even at rest. Even when you're trying to sleep. Even when your mind is calm, your body is still in drive.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium plays a direct role in regulating the nervous system, and glycinate form is particularly absorbable and gentle on the stomach. It's not a cure, but it can support the biochemical side of calming your heart rate, especially when taken consistently.

Wild, right? That something as simple as a mineral can influence your heart's resting rhythm. But that's how interconnected everything is. Your heart rate isn't just about your heart. It's about your vagus nerve, your gut microbiome, your adrenal glands, your history, your sleep quality, your unresolved trauma, your unprocessed grief. It's all in there, beating along with every pulse.

I've noticed that my heart races more at rest on days when I've been triggered, even if I didn't consciously register the trigger. A conversation that felt fine, a news story that barely registered, a dream I don't remember. My body processes things my mind misses. And it responds accordingly, with a faster heart rate, tighter shoulders, shallower breath.

How to start settling the system

You can't force your nervous system to calm down. Believe me, I've tried. I've told myself to relax, begged my heart to slow, gotten frustrated when it wouldn't listen. That approach doesn't work. The nervous system doesn't respond to commands. It responds to cues. To signals. To felt safety.

So what actually helps? Slow, intentional exhales. Longer than your inhales. Because the vagus nerve is stimulated by exhalation, and that's how you start engaging the brake. Gentle movement, like shaking or swaying, that lets your body discharge stored energy. Orienting, which means slowly looking around your environment and noticing what's actually there, not what your trauma brain expects to be there.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in Polyvagal Theory. Dana's work is grounded, accessible, and full of actual exercises you can do to map your nervous system and find your way back to regulation. It's not about achieving some perfect state of calm. It's about knowing where you are and having tools to shift.

Here's what I want you to hear. Your racing heart at rest is not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your body is doing its job based on old information. It's protecting you from a past that's over. And while that protection is exhausting, it's also understandable. It's human. It's what bodies do.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your chest loosen even slightly? Did your breath change? Did you feel a flicker of recognition, like someone finally saw what you've been carrying? That's your system responding to being understood. That's the beginning of safety.

When the body keeps saying no

Chronic heart rate elevation at rest isn't just uncomfortable. It's costly. Over time, it wears down your cardiovascular system, your immune function, your digestion, your sleep architecture. Your body wasn't designed to live in a constant state of alert. It was designed to cycle between activation and rest, between effort and recovery.

But trauma disrupts that cycle. It locks the system into activation. And then your heart keeps racing, your cortisol stays high, your inflammation increases, and your body starts to break down in ways that seem unrelated. Headaches, gut issues, chronic pain, fatigue. These are all downstream effects of a nervous system that can't find its way back to rest.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. Mate's work shows how suppressed emotions and chronic stress, especially from early trauma, create the conditions for disease. He doesn't blame the person. He shows how the body's protective responses, when they become chronic, can turn against us. It's hard reading, but necessary reading if you want to understand why your body is sounding the alarm.

Sit with that. Your heart racing at rest is your body saying no. No to the pretense that everything is fine. No to the demand that you keep going when you're exhausted. No to the expectation that you should be able to relax just because the external circumstances are safe. Your body is telling the truth. And the truth is, something needs to shift.
